# Plan — consistent --xxx / --xxx-file content flags

Design reasoning is in this task's analysis field (`endless task show E-1001 --analysis`).

## Change

For long-form content flags **text, analysis, outcome, description**: bare `--xxx` = inline,
new `--xxx-file` = path. Leave **justification** and **reason** inline-only. Remove the
`@file` magic on `--analysis`. Hard cutover, no alias.

## Surface area

- **`src/endless/cli.py`** — add `--text-file`, `--analysis-file`, `--outcome-file`,
  `--description-file`; make the bare flags inline. Affected commands include task add
  (~1205 description, ~1207 text, ~1228 justification), task update (~1276 description,
  ~1278 text, ~1290 analysis, ~1294 outcome, ~1296 justification), and `--outcome` on
  complete / decline / assume (~1351, ~1364, ~1386, ~1590). Remove `@` handling at ~1305.
- **Handlers** — `task_cmd.py` / `plan_cmd.py`: the file-read logic moves to the `--xxx-file`
  branch; bare flags pass through as literals.
- **Handoff templates** (`internal/templatecmd/templates/handoff/*.tmpl`) — change
  `research.md.tmpl`'s `--outcome <file>` → `--outcome-file <path>` (broken today). Audit the
  other three. NOTE: the `endless task show … --text` lines are the boolean *display* flag, not
  the content flag — leave those.
- **Guides / docs** — the 6 `docs/guide/*.md` (tasks, index, reference, sessions, orchestration,
  decisions), README, and any in-repo docs with `--text <file>` / `--analysis @` examples.
- **Tests** — update existing usages; add coverage that both forms work and that `--analysis @`
  no longer file-loads.

## Verification

- `endless task add/update --help` shows the split for text/analysis/outcome/description;
  justification/reason remain inline-only.
- `endless task update <t> --text 'inline'` stores the literal; `--text-file path.md` stores file
  contents; `--analysis @path` no longer file-loads.
- `grep` for `--text <path>`, `--analysis @`, `--outcome <file>` across src/, templates/, docs/,
  README finds no leftovers.
- `just test` green; spawn a task and confirm the research handoff's `--outcome-file` works.
fields.analysis:
Today the CLI's content flags are inconsistent three different ways for the same kind of
(multi-line) content:

- `--text` takes a FILE PATH (dest `text_file`) but the name reads like inline content.
- `--analysis` takes inline content OR a path via `@file` magic (added by E-1329).
- `--outcome` / `--description` take inline content only.

This forces the user to remember which flag means which, and it has already produced a latent
bug: the research handoff template instructs `endless task update … --outcome <file>`, but
`--outcome` has no file form, so the literal path is stored as the outcome text.

## Decision (locked with Mike 2026-06-20)

- Every **long-form content flag** gets BOTH forms: bare `--xxx` carries **inline** content,
  `--xxx-file` carries a **file path**. Applies to: **text, analysis, outcome, description**.
- **justification** and **reason** stay **inline-only** (typically short) — a deliberate
  partial-consistency exception, not an oversight.
- **Remove the `@file` magic** on `--analysis` (E-1329, cli.py:1305-1310); `--analysis-file`
  replaces it. One mechanism, no magic. (No auto-detect of path-vs-inline — brittleness
  outweighs convenience.)
- **Hard cutover**, no alias / migration window (pre-1.0, move-fast, no-legacy).

## Consequence to watch

`--text` today IS the file-path flag, so the change INVERTS it: `--text` becomes inline and
`--text-file` becomes the path form. Every existing `--text <path>` call site (code, templates,
docs, tests) must become `--text-file <path>`.

## Coordination (not blockers)

- E-1000 (text→plan rename): whichever name survives, the `--xxx`/`--xxx-file` split applies.
- E-1554 (adds `--notes`): should follow this convention when it lands.
- Independent of the field-separation design (E-999); applies to whichever flag names survive.
- Origin: E-989 finalization.

payload.title: Hide task show's large fields behind display flags, show char-count placeholders
payload.description: In `endless task show`, the outcome field auto-displays in full whenever a task is declined or completed, flooding the snapshot (e.g. research task E-1600's ~38KB deliverable). Make outcome flag-gated like text/analysis, and when any large field (text, analysis, outcome) is hidden, render a one-line placeholder 'Outcome: N chars (--outcome to display)' so the snapshot advertises available detail. Apply consistently across human, --llm, and --json output.

Applies to all epics, not per-epic.

fields.analysis:
# Verification-suite system — design analysis

Per ED-1509, this epic's `analysis` holds the distilled design (vision, locked decisions,
interface contracts); the forward roadmap is in the epic's plan (`--text`); the full research is
in E-1600's `outcome`.

## Vision

A product feature of Endless: every task that reaches `verify` carries a committed, executable
**verification suite** — the complete test set (unit + end-to-end) proving that task's
acceptance criteria. Runnable three ways: (a) a bare `git clone` with Endless NOT installed;
(b) `endless verify <id>` on the user's behalf; (c) an AI agent so the user needn't run it.

## Terminology

- **verification suite** — the committed, executable proof of a task's acceptance criteria
  (unit + e2e). (Avoids "artifact," which in CI/CD means a build output.)
- **test runner** — the execution engine that runs the suite (`go test`, `pytest`, `bats`,
  testscript). Endless is never the test runner; it discovers suites, invokes the runner,
  isolates the run, normalizes results, presents pass/fail. (Avoids "runtime.")

## Load-bearing constraint

Endless must require NO external tool to use the core feature. Requiring mise/Docker/etc. would
itself be a reason not to adopt Endless. Heavier tooling must never become a precondition; the
design only must not block adding it later.

## Design principles

1. Triple-runnable (bare clone with no Endless / by Endless / by an agent).
2. Discoverer/orchestrator, never the test runner — convention + manifest, not a dependency.
3. Native runners underneath + a uniform discovery/result contract on top.
4. The suite is ALL testing for the task's scope (unit + e2e); may be slow when needed. Tiering
   = when you run (smoke vs full), not what's included.
5. CLI-only is a PRO — lets Endless drive tools invisibly.
6. All-Go future: no Python/uv lock-in.
7. Not a CI/CD platform. Hermetic builds / production parity are user-owned.
8. The chosen form must be trivially authorable by an AI agent with minimal-to-zero user input.

## Locked decisions (2026-06-20, post E-1600 research)

1. **Result contract = CTRF schema, subset implementation, vendored writer.** Implement only the
   CTRF fields we need, field-names/shapes matching CTRF, pinned to the CTRF snapshot
   (2025-11-24). Read native producers ourselves; NO runtime dependency on external CTRF
   reporters. (CTRF is pre-1.0/single-maintainer — we own the writer so upstream churn can't
   reach us; "CTRF-shaped" keeps later interop free.)
2. **Suite location = `.endless/tasks/<id>/`** — a product-controlled directory, so the
   convention is identical across every project regardless of its `tests/` layout.
3. **Scope = Stages 1–2 now; defer Stage 3 (containers) and Stage 4 (PTY/tmux).** Dogfood on
   Endless (the easy Tier-0 case) first.
4. **Build inside Endless, keep the seam clean** for possible later extraction; no extraction
   cost now beyond clean boundaries.

Note (general, not just here): the temp `XDG_CONFIG_HOME` + temp SQLite Tier-0 trick is the
zero-dependency default ONLY when the system-under-test is Endless (or a project whose entire
state is a temp dir). Arbitrary projects with service dependencies default to Tier 3
(containers), selected by the suite's declared `needs`. This does not violate principle 2 — the
core never *requires* a tool; `needs` simply selects the substrate a given suite asks for.

## Interface Contract (foundation trio E-1602/E-1603/E-1604 MUST conform)

The three foundation children define interlocking interfaces. They are pinned here so the
children cannot diverge.

### A. The `verify.toml` manifest (E-1602 owns the schema; E-1603 reads it)

Location: `.endless/tasks/<id>/verify.toml`, beside the suite files and an optional `fixtures/`.
Discovery: glob `.endless/tasks/*/verify.toml`. TOML (comments; no YAML coercion footguns;
Go-idiomatic). Kept flat by design.

```toml
schema = 1
task   = "E-1234"
runner = "go test ./.endless/tasks/E-1234/..."   # the command a bare clone runs directly
format = "gotest-json"                            # gotest-json | pytest-json | tap
tiers  = ["smoke", "full"]                        # optional
seed   = ["fixtures/baseline.json"]               # optional (E-1606)
needs  = []                                       # optional; selects isolation tier (Stage 3+)
```

The manifest is a POINTER, not a spec: it names the runner/format/needs and never re-describes
the tests. The suite body stays in the native runner. No standardized cross-language spec layer
(that would reinvent Gherkin's two-artifact drift).

### B. Runner → result dataflow (E-1603 produces, E-1604 consumes)

1. E-1603 reads the manifest, creates a per-run temp dir, exports `XDG_CONFIG_HOME=<tmp>`, points
   the task DB at a fresh temp SQLite file (Tier 0).
2. E-1603 invokes the `runner` string, capturing exit code + the native result stream named by
   `format`.
3. E-1603 passes (raw native stream, `format`) to E-1604's normalizer.
4. E-1603 tears down the temp dir (honor a `--keep` debug flag).

### C. The CTRF-subset envelope (E-1604 owns — the stable output contract)

A single JSON document (UTF-8). Subset we implement now:

- Top: `results.tool.name`; `results.summary { tests, passed, failed, skipped, pending, other,
  start, stop }`; `results.tests[]`.
- Per test: required `name`, `status` (`passed|failed|skipped|pending|other`), `duration` (ms);
  optional `message`, `trace`, `suite`, `stdout`, `stderr`, `attachments[]`
  (`{ name, contentType, path }` — the non-text hook: screenshots/HTTP transcripts), `extra`.
- Field names/shapes match CTRF; emit only this subset; ignore the rest until needed. Endless
  WRITES this itself from the native stream; no external CTRF reporter at runtime.

### D. Exit-code contract (bare-clone mode)

The `runner` string alone must exit 0 on all-pass and non-zero on any failure, with no Endless
present. CTRF normalization and seeding are Endless-only enhancements unavailable in bare mode;
the runner string must be self-sufficient.

## Lessons from ad-hoc prototypes

`tests/tasks/e-1577-verify.sh` and `tests/tasks/e-1570-verify.sh` are reference-only prototypes
(not deliverables). Lessons, now folded into the design: (a) the sandbox lacked a supported
seeding verb (E-1570's author reached into `endless-go session-query record-bg-agent`) → E-1606
adds first-class seeding; (b) two happy paths were left MANUAL because they `exec` into
`claude attach` or need a live tmux → Stage 4 (deferred) automates these via PTY/disposable
container.

fields.text:
# Plan — verify.toml manifest schema + discovery convention

Shared interface contract is in the epic's analysis (`endless task show E-1596 --analysis`,
section "Interface Contract / A"). This plan implements section A. Do not re-derive the schema —
conform to it.

## Deliver

1. **The `verify.toml` schema** (flat TOML): `schema`, `task`, `runner`, `format`
   (`gotest-json|pytest-json|tap`), optional `tiers`, `seed`, `needs`. Parse with a Go TOML lib
   (`BurntSushi/toml` or `pelletier/go-toml`). Define a Go struct + validation (required fields,
   known `format` values).
2. **The location/layout convention:** `.endless/tasks/<id>/verify.toml` beside suite files and
   optional `fixtures/`.
3. **Discovery:** a function that globs `.endless/tasks/*/verify.toml` and returns parsed
   manifests keyed by task id; reused by `endless verify` (E-1603).

## Notes

- Manifest is a pointer, not a spec (per the contract) — no test-description fields.
- Keep the struct flat; if a future need pushes nesting, re-evaluate TOML rather than nest.
- All-Go: this is Go code (the migration target), even though the current CLI is Python — put it
  where E-1603's `endless verify` will consume it.

## Verify

- A sample `.endless/tasks/E-XXXX/verify.toml` parses into the struct; missing required field or
  unknown `format` fails validation loudly.
- Discovery finds all manifests under `.endless/tasks/*/` and ignores unrelated files.
- Unit tests cover parse + validation + discovery glob.

# Plan — endless verify Tier-0 runner

Shared interface contract is in the epic's analysis (`endless task show E-1596 --analysis`,
sections "Interface Contract / B" and "/ D"). Conform to it; don't re-derive the dataflow.

## Deliver

`endless verify <id>` (and `endless verify` for the cwd's task), Tier 0 only:

1. Resolve the task's manifest via E-1602's discovery.
2. Create a per-run temp dir; export `XDG_CONFIG_HOME=<tmp>`; point the task DB at a fresh temp
   SQLite file (reuse the existing per-worktree sandbox mechanism — see CLAUDE.md "Self-dev DB
   sandbox" and `conftest.py`'s `isolated_env` for the established temp-XDG pattern).
3. Invoke the manifest's `runner` string in that environment; capture stdout/stderr, exit code,
   and the native result stream identified by `format`.
4. Hand (raw native stream, `format`) to E-1604's normalizer; print pass/fail summary + the CTRF
   path. Exit 0 on all-pass, non-zero otherwise.
5. Tear down the temp dir; honor `--keep` to retain it for debugging (mirrors testscript
   `-testwork`).

## Boundaries

- Tier 0 only. `needs`-driven escalation (Tiers 2–4) is later stages; if `needs` is non-empty,
  fail loudly with "tier not yet supported" rather than silently running unisolated.
- Concurrency-safety comes from per-run temp `XDG_CONFIG_HOME` + DB file (multiple-sandbox
  hardening is E-1608).
- Depends on E-1602 (manifest/discovery) and E-1604 (normalizer).

## Verify

- `endless verify <id>` on a task whose suite passes → exit 0, CTRF written, summary shown.
- A deliberately failing suite → non-zero exit, failure detail in the summary.
- Two concurrent runs in one worktree don't collide (separate temp DBs).
- `--keep` leaves the temp dir; default removes it.

# Plan — vendored CTRF-subset result writer + native normalizers

Shared interface contract is in the epic's analysis (`endless task show E-1596 --analysis`,
section "Interface Contract / C" and locked decision 1). Conform to the CTRF-subset shape; don't
invent fields.

## Deliver

1. **A Go CTRF-subset writer** — structs for the subset in decision/contract C: `results.tool`,
   `results.summary {tests,passed,failed,skipped,pending,other,start,stop}`, `results.tests[]`
   with per-test `name`/`status`/`duration` (+ optional `message`, `trace`, `suite`,
   `stdout`/`stderr`, `attachments[] {name,contentType,path}`, `extra`). Field names/shapes
   match CTRF; pin to the CTRF snapshot (2025-11-24). Emit a single JSON document.
2. **Native normalizers** keyed by `format`:
   - `gotest-json`: parse the `go test -json` TestEvent stream (Action/Test/Elapsed/Output).
   - `pytest-json`: parse pytest's JSON report.
   - `tap`: parse TAP (for shell/BATS suites).
   Each maps native → the CTRF-subset structs.
3. **No runtime dependency on external CTRF reporters** — Endless reads the native streams and
   writes CTRF itself. (Don't add `go-ctrf-json-reporter`/`pytest-json-ctrf` as runtime deps.)

## Notes

- Keep the subset minimal; add CTRF fields only when a concrete need appears.
- The `attachments[]`/`extra` hooks exist for non-text outcomes (screenshots, HTTP transcripts)
  but need no producer yet.
- Consumed by E-1603; used to render expected/got failure detail.

## Verify

- Golden tests: a known `go test -json` stream, a pytest JSON sample, and a TAP sample each
  normalize to the expected CTRF-subset JSON.
- Failures carry `message`/`trace`; summary counts match the native totals.
- Output validates as a single JSON object with the pinned field names.

# Implement ED-1509: epic child handoffs read parent analysis + guide section

Implements ED-1509 (epic content-field convention). Two pieces, both applying to ALL epics.

## 1. Handoff: a child of an epic is pointed at the parent's analysis

When a spawned task's parent is an `epic`, its handoff must instruct the child to read the
parent epic's shared design/contracts:

`endless task show E-<parent> --analysis --db main`

fields.analysis:
# Verification-suite system — design analysis

Per ED-1509, this epic's `analysis` holds the distilled design (vision, locked decisions,
interface contracts); the forward roadmap is in the epic's plan (`--text`); the full research is
in E-1600's `outcome`.

## Vision

A product feature of Endless: every task that reaches `verify` carries a committed, executable
**verification suite** — the complete test set (unit + end-to-end) proving that task's
acceptance criteria. Runnable three ways: (a) a bare `git clone` with Endless NOT installed;
(b) `endless verify <id>` on the user's behalf; (c) an AI agent so the user needn't run it.

## Terminology

- **verification suite** — the committed, executable proof of a task's acceptance criteria
  (unit + e2e). (Avoids "artifact," which in CI/CD means a build output.)
- **test runner** — the execution engine that runs the suite (`go test`, `pytest`, `bats`,
  testscript). Endless is never the test runner; it discovers suites, invokes the runner,
  isolates the run, normalizes results, presents pass/fail. (Avoids "runtime.")

## Load-bearing constraint

Endless must require NO external tool to use the core feature. Requiring mise/Docker/etc. would
itself be a reason not to adopt Endless. Heavier tooling must never become a precondition; the
design only must not block adding it later.

## Design principles

1. Triple-runnable (bare clone with no Endless / by Endless / by an agent).
2. Discoverer/orchestrator, never the test runner — convention + manifest, not a dependency.
3. Native runners underneath + a uniform discovery/result contract on top.
4. The suite is ALL testing for the task's scope (unit + e2e); may be slow when needed. Tiering
   = when you run (smoke vs full), not what's included.
5. CLI-only is a PRO — lets Endless drive tools invisibly.
6. All-Go future: no Python/uv lock-in.
7. Not a CI/CD platform. Hermetic builds / production parity are user-owned.
8. The chosen form must be trivially authorable by an AI agent with minimal-to-zero user input.

## Locked decisions (2026-06-20, post E-1600 research)

1. **Result contract = CTRF schema, subset implementation, vendored writer.** Implement only the
   CTRF fields we need, field-names/shapes matching CTRF, pinned to the CTRF snapshot
   (2025-11-24). Read native producers ourselves; NO runtime dependency on external CTRF
   reporters. (CTRF is pre-1.0/single-maintainer — we own the writer so upstream churn can't
   reach us; "CTRF-shaped" keeps later interop free.)
2. **Suite location = `.endless/tasks/<id>/`** — a product-controlled directory, so the
   convention is identical across every project regardless of its `tests/` layout.
3. **Scope = Stages 1–2 now; defer Stage 3 (containers) and Stage 4 (PTY/tmux).** Dogfood on
   Endless (the easy Tier-0 case) first.
4. **Build inside Endless, keep the seam clean** for possible later extraction; no extraction
   cost now beyond clean boundaries.

Note (general): the **product** Tier-0 substrate is a fresh temp working dir + isolated env
(temp `HOME`/`XDG_CONFIG_HOME`) — zero-dependency and app-agnostic. The temp-SQLite "fresh task
DB" is NOT part of the product runner; it is incidental to Endless being the system-under-test
(Endless's DB lives under XDG, so XDG isolation supplies it automatically). Tier 0 fits any
project whose state is contained by a temp dir; arbitrary projects with service dependencies
default to Tier 3 (containers), selected by the suite's declared `needs`. This does not violate
principle 2 — the core never *requires* a tool; `needs` selects the substrate a suite asks for.

## Interface Contract (foundation trio E-1602/E-1603/E-1604 MUST conform)

The three foundation children define interlocking interfaces. They are pinned here so the
children cannot diverge.

### A. The `verify.toml` manifest (E-1602 owns the schema; E-1603 reads it)

Location: `.endless/tasks/<id>/verify.toml`, beside the suite files and an optional `fixtures/`.
Discovery: glob `.endless/tasks/*/verify.toml`. TOML (comments; no YAML coercion footguns;
Go-idiomatic). Kept flat by design.

```toml
schema = 1
task   = "E-1234"
runner = "go test ./.endless/tasks/E-1234/..."   # the command a bare clone runs directly
format = "gotest-json"                            # gotest-json | pytest-json | tap
tiers  = ["smoke", "full"]                        # optional
seed   = ["fixtures/baseline.json"]               # optional (E-1606)
needs  = []                                       # optional; selects isolation tier (Stage 3+)
```

The manifest is a POINTER, not a spec: it names the runner/format/needs and never re-describes
the tests. The suite body stays in the native runner. No standardized cross-language spec layer
(that would reinvent Gherkin's two-artifact drift).

### B. Runner → result dataflow (E-1603 produces, E-1604 consumes)

1. E-1603 reads the manifest and creates **generic, app-agnostic per-run isolation**: a fresh
   temp working dir + an isolated env (temp `HOME`, temp `XDG_CONFIG_HOME`) so the suite can't
   touch the developer's real home/config (Tier 0). The runner has NO application-specific state
   knowledge (no task DB, no SQLite). *Self_dev note:* since Endless's own DB lives under
   `XDG_CONFIG_HOME`, isolating XDG gives an Endless-SUT suite a fresh DB for free — incidental,
   not runner logic.
2. E-1603 invokes the `runner` string, capturing exit code + the native result stream named by
   `format`.
3. E-1603 passes (raw native stream, `format`) to E-1604's normalizer.
4. E-1603 tears down the temp dir (honor a `--keep` debug flag).

### C. The CTRF-subset envelope (E-1604 owns — the stable output contract)

A single JSON document (UTF-8). Subset we implement now:

- Top: `results.tool.name`; `results.summary { tests, passed, failed, skipped, pending, other,
  start, stop }`; `results.tests[]`.
- Per test: required `name`, `status` (`passed|failed|skipped|pending|other`), `duration` (ms);
  optional `message`, `trace`, `suite`, `stdout`, `stderr`, `attachments[]`
  (`{ name, contentType, path }` — the non-text hook: screenshots/HTTP transcripts), `extra`.
- Field names/shapes match CTRF; emit only this subset; ignore the rest until needed. Endless
  WRITES this itself from the native stream; no external CTRF reporter at runtime.

### D. Exit-code contract (bare-clone mode)

The `runner` string alone must exit 0 on all-pass and non-zero on any failure, with no Endless
present. CTRF normalization and seeding are Endless-only enhancements unavailable in bare mode;
the runner string must be self-sufficient.

## Lessons from ad-hoc prototypes

`tests/tasks/e-1577-verify.sh` and `tests/tasks/e-1570-verify.sh` are reference-only prototypes
(not deliverables). Lessons, now folded into the design: (a) the sandbox lacked a supported
seeding verb (E-1570's author reached into `endless-go session-query record-bg-agent`) → E-1606
adds first-class seeding; (b) two happy paths were left MANUAL because they `exec` into
`claude attach` or need a live tmux → Stage 4 (deferred) automates these via PTY/disposable
container.

# Plan — endless verify Tier-0 runner

Shared interface contract is in the epic's analysis (`endless task show E-1596 --analysis`,
sections "Interface Contract / B" and "/ D"). Conform to it; don't re-derive the dataflow.

## Deliver

`endless verify <id>` (and `endless verify` for the cwd's task), Tier 0 only:

1. Resolve the task's manifest via E-1602's discovery.
2. **Generic per-run isolation (product-level, app-agnostic):** create a fresh per-run temp
   working directory and run the suite under an isolated environment — a temp `HOME` and temp
   `XDG_CONFIG_HOME` — so the suite cannot read or pollute the developer's real home/config.
   This is the zero-dependency Tier-0 substrate and it knows NOTHING about any specific
   application's state.
3. Invoke the manifest's `runner` string in that environment; capture stdout/stderr, exit code,
   and the native result stream identified by `format`.
4. Hand (raw native stream, `format`) to E-1604's normalizer; print pass/fail summary + the CTRF
   path. Exit 0 on all-pass, non-zero otherwise.
5. Tear down the temp dir; honor `--keep` to retain it for debugging (mirrors testscript
   `-testwork`).

## Boundaries

- Tier 0 only. `needs`-driven escalation (Tiers 2–4) is later stages; if `needs` is non-empty,
  fail loudly with "tier not yet supported" rather than silently running unisolated.
- **PRODUCT vs self_dev — keep them separate.** The runner provides ONLY generic env +
  working-dir isolation. It must contain NO Endless-specific state handling — no "task DB", no
  SQLite knowledge. Application-specific preconditions are the suite's responsibility (E-1606
  seeding / fixtures, or the project's own setup).
- **Self_dev note (NOT product behavior):** because Endless stores its own DB under
  `XDG_CONFIG_HOME`, isolating XDG automatically gives an Endless-as-SUT suite a fresh DB for
  free — no special-casing in the runner. The per-worktree sandbox (CLAUDE.md "Self-dev DB
  sandbox") and `conftest.py`'s `isolated_env` are reference patterns for that self_dev case
  only, not requirements of the product runner.
- Concurrency-safety comes from each run's own temp working dir + isolated env (multiple-sandbox
  hardening for the self_dev case is E-1608).
- Depends on E-1602 (manifest/discovery) and E-1604 (normalizer).

## Verify

- `endless verify <id>` on a task whose suite passes → exit 0, CTRF written, summary shown.
- A deliberately failing suite → non-zero exit, failure detail in the summary.
- The suite runs with `HOME`/`XDG_CONFIG_HOME` pointed at temp dirs — confirm it cannot see the
  developer's real config.
- Two concurrent runs don't collide.
- `--keep` leaves the temp dir; default removes it.

fields.title: Build endless verify Tier-0 runner with generic per-run env and working-dir isolation

Generic and app-agnostic — no application-specific state knowledge (no task DB / SQLite). Zero external dependencies (Tier 0).

fields.text:
# E-1572 — Add soft warning at endless task spawn --bg when N bg agents already active

Lands after **E-1568** (background-kind rows exist) and **E-1571** (`kind_id` FK column populated). Independent of E-1567/E-1570.

## Decisions

### Threshold and config
- Default: `3`. Configurable as `bg_throttle_warn` in `.endless/config.json`.
- Config loader: read at spawn time via `config.project_config_read(config.resolution_cwd())` (Python). Pure read; no write path.
- Disable mechanism: set `bg_throttle_warn` to `0` (or any value `<= 0`) — the warning fires only when `active_count >= threshold AND threshold > 0`.

### Count query — lives in Go, NOT Python
Per E-1486 / E-894, new DB reads go through the Go `session-query` helper, not Python `db.query`. (The package docstring states this is its purpose; the sibling `_lookup_bg_short_id` predates the rule and is not a precedent to extend.)

- New Go subcommand: `endless-go session-query count-bg-agents --task-id <id>` → prints the integer count.
- Backed by `monitor.CountActiveBgAgents(taskID)`, which resolves `project_id` from the task Go-side (mirroring `RecordBgAgentSession`, so the Python flow needs no DB read) and runs:

```sql
SELECT count(*) FROM sessions
WHERE kind_id = ?            -- int64(sessionkind.SessionKindBackground)
  AND state = 'working'
  AND project_id = ?
```

The `kind_id` filter uses the typed `sessionkind.SessionKindBackground` constant rather than a hardcoded integer or a `session_kinds` subselect — the Go enum is the stable reference. Scope is **per project**, not global: bg agents in unrelated projects do not consume this project's mental budget. `tasks.project_id` is `NOT NULL`, so the resolution is a plain scan.

### Trigger and behavior
At dispatch time, AFTER status gate / worktree pre-claim but BEFORE invoking `claude --bg` (in `_spawn_bg_dispatch`, immediately before the `claude --bg` subprocess):
- Call `_bg_throttle_warn(item_id)`: read the threshold from project config, get the count from the Go helper.
- If `active_count >= threshold` (and `threshold > 0`), emit a 3-line warning to **stderr** via `click.echo(..., err=True)` (not stdout — keeps short-id parsing on stdout clean if a caller pipes us):

```
warning: {active_count} bg agents already active for this project (threshold: {threshold}).
  Each bg agent consumes a parallel-execution slot; quota burns ~linearly.
  Community-observed sweet spot is 3–5 parallel agents. (Configure via .endless/config.json:bg_throttle_warn.)
```

- **Does not block.** Proceed with dispatch unconditionally. Coordinator decides.
- **Advisory-only robustness:** `_bg_throttle_warn` never raises on its own failure — a config or count-helper hiccup (non-zero exit, unparseable output) is swallowed silently so it can never abort the spawn.
- No prompt-for-confirmation (would break automation; soft warning is the point).

### Rationale (for the implementer)
Per `docs/research-2026-06-12-claude-background-agents.md` §7 — bg quota burns linearly with parallel count; community sweet spot is 3–5. Threshold default of 3 leaves headroom before hitting the upper community-observed limit while warning early.

### Suppression
No `--no-warn` flag. If automation needs silence, set `bg_throttle_warn` to `0` in `.endless/config.json`. Per-invocation suppression would just be reinventing log-level filtering.

## Files

### Changed (Go)
- `internal/monitor/session.go` — add `CountActiveBgAgents(taskID int64) (int64, error)`.
- `internal/sessionquerycmd/session_query.go` — add the `count-bg-agents` subcommand (`runCountBgAgents`) + usage line.

### Changed (Python)
- `src/endless/task_cmd.py` — add `_bg_throttle_warn(item_id)` (threshold read + Go-helper count + stderr warning) and call it inside `_spawn_bg_dispatch`, between pre-claim and the `claude --bg` subprocess call.

### Tests
- `internal/monitor/count_bg_agents_test.go` (new) — count-query correctness: scopes to project + kind=background + state='working'; excludes ended sessions, foreground sessions, and other projects; returns 0 when none active.
- `tests/test_spawn_bg_throttle.py` (new) — warning-decision + text (count helper mocked):
  - Threshold 3, 2 active → no warning.
  - Threshold 3, 3 active → warning emitted to stderr.
  - Threshold 3, 5 active → warning emitted once (not per-overage).
  - Threshold 0 / negative → no warning regardless of count.
  - Unset key → default 3.
  - Warning text contains the count and threshold.
  - Count-helper non-zero exit → silent (no warning, no raise).
- `tests/test_spawn_bg.py` — existing dispatch-mechanics tests stub `_bg_throttle_warn` to a no-op so the new pre-dispatch count shell-out doesn't perturb them.

## Sequence note

Lands after **E-1568** (the rows it counts didn't exist before).

## Verification

1. `just test` (Python) and `go test ./internal/monitor/ ./internal/sessionquerycmd/` pass.
2. `bin/endless-go --config-dir ~/.config/endless session-query count-bg-agents --task-id <id>` prints an integer.
3. Set `bg_throttle_warn: 1` in `.endless/config.json`. Dispatch one bg agent → no warning. Dispatch a second → warning text appears on stderr, dispatch still succeeds.
4. Unset / set to 0 → no warning at any count.

payload.title: Add setup field and project-level .endless/verify.toml config layering to the manifest
payload.description: E-1602 landed the verify.toml manifest (runner/format/tiers/seed/needs) but not environment preparation. Add a 'setup' field (the third precondition kind: build/install/migrate, run before seed) and a project-level .endless/verify.toml config layer merged with per-task manifests, so shared setup (build + schema init) is not duplicated per task. Standard script home: .endless/verify/ (project-shared) and each task's .endless/tasks dir.
payload.text:
# Plan — setup field + project-level verify config layering

Adds environment **preparation** to the verify manifest — the gap E-1602 (landed) didn't cover.
Shared design is in the epic's analysis (`endless task show E-1596 --analysis`, Interface
Contract). Conform to it.

## The three precondition kinds (taxonomy)

- `needs` → **provision the substrate** (what must exist: toolchain, services). Already in the
  manifest; satisfied by the isolation tier.
- **`setup`** → **prepare the project** (build / install / migrate / codegen). THIS task adds it.
- `seed` → **load state/fixtures** (E-1606).

Run order (executed by the E-1603 runner): **provision → setup → seed → run → teardown.**

## Deliver

1. **`setup` field** in the manifest schema (extends E-1602's struct): an ordered list of
   commands and/or references to script files. Runs after provisioning, before seed. A failing
   setup step aborts the run loudly, naming the step.
2. **Project-level config `.endless/verify.toml`**: holds shared `setup`, default
   `needs`/`format`, shared fixtures. Merged with each per-task manifest under
   `.endless/tasks/`. Merge semantics: project-level setup runs first, then per-task setup
   appends (per-task may override defaults). Define + document the merge precisely; reuse
   Endless's layered-config approach (go-cfgstore / `.endless/config.json`).
3. **Standard script locations**: `.endless/verify/` for project-shared scripts (e.g.
   `.endless/verify/setup.sh`); per-task scripts beside the task manifest. Short steps may inline
   in TOML; non-trivial setup lives in real script files (liner/editor-friendly).

## Generic vs Endless (no PRODUCT/self_dev leak)

The `setup` field is generic; the *commands* are project-specific. Endless's own setup (build the
worktree binaries + initialize schema into the fresh temp env) lives in Endless's project-level
`.endless/verify.toml`, NOT in product code.

## Verify

- A project-level `.endless/verify.toml` + a per-task manifest merge into the expected effective
  config (project setup then task setup).
- Setup steps run in order before the runner; a failing step aborts loudly with the step named.
- Endless's own `.endless/verify.toml` prepares a fresh temp env so a dogfood suite runs green.

## Deps

- Follows E-1602 (landed manifest schema/discovery). Blocks E-1603 (the runner executes `setup`).

```toml
tiers  = ["smoke", "full"]                        # optional
seed   = ["fixtures/baseline.json"]               # optional (E-1606)
needs  = []                                       # optional; selects isolation tier (Stage 3+)
```

The manifest is a POINTER, not a spec: it names runner/format/setup/seed/needs and never
re-describes the tests. The suite body stays in the native runner. No standardized cross-language
spec layer (that would reinvent Gherkin's two-artifact drift).

**Three precondition kinds** (do not conflate): `needs` = provision the substrate (what must
exist); `setup` = prepare the project (build/install/migrate/codegen); `seed` = load
state/fixtures. Standard script homes: `.endless/verify/` (project-shared, e.g.
`.endless/verify/setup.sh`) and each task's `.endless/tasks/<id>/` dir; short steps may inline.

### B. Runner → result dataflow (E-1603 produces, E-1604 consumes)

1. E-1603 reads the manifest and creates **generic, app-agnostic per-run isolation**: a fresh
   temp working dir + an isolated env (temp `HOME`, temp `XDG_CONFIG_HOME`) so the suite can't
   touch the developer's real home/config (Tier 0). The runner has NO application-specific state
   knowledge (no task DB, no SQLite). *Self_dev note:* since Endless's own DB lives under
   `XDG_CONFIG_HOME`, isolating XDG gives an Endless-SUT suite a fresh DB for free — incidental,
   not runner logic.
2. E-1603 runs preconditions in order: provision (Tier-0 no-op) → merged `setup` steps (abort
   loudly on failure) → `seed` (E-1606).
3. E-1603 invokes the `runner` string, capturing exit code + the native result stream named by
   `format`.
4. E-1603 passes (raw native stream, `format`) to E-1604's normalizer.
5. E-1603 tears down the temp dir (honor a `--keep` debug flag).

### C. The CTRF-subset envelope (E-1604 owns — the stable output contract)

A single JSON document (UTF-8). Subset we implement now:

- Top: `results.tool.name`; `results.summary { tests, passed, failed, skipped, pending, other,
  start, stop }`; `results.tests[]`.
- Per test: required `name`, `status` (`passed|failed|skipped|pending|other`), `duration` (ms);
  optional `message`, `trace`, `suite`, `stdout`, `stderr`, `attachments[]`
  (`{ name, contentType, path }` — the non-text hook: screenshots/HTTP transcripts), `extra`.
- Field names/shapes match CTRF; emit only this subset; ignore the rest until needed. Endless
  WRITES this itself from the native stream; no external CTRF reporter at runtime.

### D. Exit-code contract (bare-clone mode)

The `runner` string alone must exit 0 on all-pass and non-zero on any failure, with no Endless
present. CTRF normalization and seeding are Endless-only enhancements unavailable in bare mode;
the runner string must be self-sufficient.

## Lessons from ad-hoc prototypes

`tests/tasks/e-1577-verify.sh` and `tests/tasks/e-1570-verify.sh` are reference-only prototypes
(not deliverables). Lessons, now folded into the design: (a) the sandbox lacked a supported
seeding verb (E-1570's author reached into `endless-go session-query record-bg-agent`) → E-1606
adds first-class seeding; (b) two happy paths were left MANUAL because they `exec` into
`claude attach` or need a live tmux → Stage 4 (deferred) automates these via PTY/disposable
container.

payload.title: Harden verify framework against false greens from swallow-on-failure advisory code
payload.description: Verify suites can report ALL PASSED while the feature is silently broken: advisory code that swallows its own failures (e.g. E-1572's _bg_throttle_warn) makes 'correctly did nothing' indistinguishable from 'silently broke', and dependency resolution can pick a stale global binary. Recommends an ENDLESS_GO_BIN injection seam, a debug toggle that surfaces swallowed errors, mandatory positive controls paired with every silence assertion, and a tripwire meta-check. Surfaced while writing tests/tasks/e-1572-verify.sh.

fields.text:
# Finding — verify suites give false greens for swallow-on-failure advisory code

Input for the E-1596 verification-suite framework, surfaced while writing the ad-hoc verify
script for E-1572 (`tests/tasks/e-1572-verify.sh`). Captures a class of verification blind spot
and recommends framework primitives + a house rule to close it.

## The hazard

Two factors combine; either alone is benign.

**1. The swallow collapses two observables into one.** Advisory / best-effort code (E-1572's
`_bg_throttle_warn` is the example) deliberately swallows its own failures — a config error, a
non-zero exit from a helper subprocess, or unparseable output all return silently, because the
feature must never abort the caller. That is correct production behavior. But it means
**"correctly did nothing" and "silently broke" produce the identical observable** (here: empty
stderr). A verify check that asserts *absence* (`threshold 0 → no warning`, `below threshold →
no warning`) passes in both worlds. A suite built only of absence assertions is therefore
satisfiable by *total* breakage of the feature — green when the underlying path is completely
dead.

**2. Dependency resolution can silently pick the wrong artifact.** `_resolve_endless_go()` falls
back to `shutil.which("endless-go")` — the global install, which predates the new
`count-bg-agents` subcommand. Run the Python path naively and the count subprocess exits
non-zero ("unknown subcommand")... which factor #1 then swallows into silence.

Multiplied: a verify suite can exercise stale code through a broken dependency and still report
ALL PASSED. The E-1572 script only avoided this because it also contains *positive* checks
(`at threshold → warning present`) that go red the instant the count path breaks. That positive
assertion is load-bearing — but today it is load-bearing by how the author happened to write it,
not by any framework rule.

Subtler: the E-1572 script defended factor #2 by **monkeypatching a private function**
(`eb._resolve_endless_go = ...`) inside the snippet. That mitigation is itself fragile — rename
the function and the patch silently no-ops, resolution falls back to the global, and factor #1
makes it green again. The mitigation shares the failure mode it mitigates.

## Recommendations (by leverage)

**1. Give dependency resolution an injection seam (highest leverage).** `_resolve_endless_go`
should honor an explicit override (e.g. `ENDLESS_GO_BIN`) before any PATH/global fallback. Verify
scripts then pin the artifact-under-test with an env var instead of monkeypatching internals —
robust across renames, and useful beyond testing. Needing to reach into a private function is the
smell that this seam is missing.

**2. Make the swallow observable under a debug toggle (systemic fix for factor #1).** Advisory
code that swallows should, under a debug toggle (e.g. `ENDLESS_DEBUG`), emit the swallowed error
to stderr/a log while still not changing control flow. Production stays silent and non-blocking;
verify runs with the toggle and asserts *"the dependency path did not error,"* converting the
blind spot into a first-class observable. House-rule candidate: **swallow in production, surface
the swallow on demand.** Smallest change that dissolves the core ambiguity; also helps real
debugging.

**3. Make a positive control mandatory whenever a suite asserts silence.** In the result-contract
/ assertion vocabulary, distinguish `assert_silent` from `assert_emitted` and document that
**`assert_silent` is necessary-but-not-sufficient for swallow-on-failure code** — every absence
claim must be paired with a positive check driving the *same dependency chain* to a loud
observable. A suite of pure-absence checks over this class of code should be a lint failure.

**4. Add a tripwire meta-check (proves the canary is wired).** One cheap check that *deliberately*
breaks the dependency — point `ENDLESS_GO_BIN` at `/nonexistent`, rerun the positive scenario,
assert the observable *disappears*. Proves the positive assertion actually flows through the real
dependency rather than passing for an unrelated reason. Generalizes: **negative-control your
positive controls.**

Ranking: #1 and #2 are durable fixes (remove the trap); #3 and #4 are the discipline that catches
it when the trap reappears. If only one lands first, make it #2.

## Disposition

Findings + recommendations are the deliverable here. Implementation (the `ENDLESS_GO_BIN` seam,
the debug toggle, the assertion-vocabulary rules) belongs to the E-1596 framework build and may be
split into its own task(s) by the owning session — not done here.

# Plan — endless verify Tier-0 runner

Shared interface contract is in the epic's analysis (`endless task show E-1596 --analysis`,
sections "Interface Contract / B" and "/ D"). Conform to it; don't re-derive the dataflow.

## Deliver

`endless verify <id>` (and `endless verify` for the cwd's task), Tier 0 only:

1. Resolve the task's manifest via E-1602's discovery.
2. **Generic per-run isolation (product-level, app-agnostic):** create a fresh per-run temp
   working directory and run the suite under an isolated environment — a temp `HOME` and temp
   `XDG_CONFIG_HOME` — so the suite cannot read or pollute the developer's real home/config.
   This is the zero-dependency Tier-0 substrate and it knows NOTHING about any specific
   application's state.
3. **Run preconditions in order** before testing: provision (Tier-0 = no-op) → run the merged
   `setup` steps (from the project + task config; aborts loudly on a failing step) → apply `seed`
   (E-1606). The `setup` field + merged config come from the setup/config-layering task.
4. Invoke the manifest's `runner` string in that environment; capture stdout/stderr, exit code,
   and the native result stream identified by `format`.
5. Hand (raw native stream, `format`) to E-1604's normalizer; print pass/fail summary + the CTRF
   path. Exit 0 on all-pass, non-zero otherwise.
6. Tear down the temp dir; honor `--keep` to retain it for debugging (mirrors testscript
   `-testwork`).

## Boundaries

- Tier 0 only. `needs`-driven escalation (Tiers 2–4) is later stages; if `needs` is non-empty,
  fail loudly with "tier not yet supported" rather than silently running unisolated.
- **PRODUCT vs self_dev — keep them separate.** The runner provides ONLY generic env +
  working-dir isolation. It must contain NO Endless-specific state handling — no "task DB", no
  SQLite knowledge. Application-specific preconditions are the suite's responsibility (E-1606
  seeding / fixtures, or the project's own setup).
- **Self_dev note (NOT product behavior):** because Endless stores its own DB under
  `XDG_CONFIG_HOME`, isolating XDG automatically gives an Endless-as-SUT suite a fresh DB for
  free — no special-casing in the runner. The per-worktree sandbox (CLAUDE.md "Self-dev DB
  sandbox") and `conftest.py`'s `isolated_env` are reference patterns for that self_dev case
  only, not requirements of the product runner.
- Concurrency-safety comes from each run's own temp working dir + isolated env (multiple-sandbox
  hardening for the self_dev case is E-1608).
- Depends on E-1602 (manifest/discovery, landed), E-1604 (normalizer), and the setup/config-
  layering task (provides the `setup` field + merged project/task config this runner executes).

## Verify — ad-hoc verify script (interim, E-1577 style)

Until the formal verification system this epic builds is usable, verify this task with a
committed ad-hoc script `tests/tasks/e-1603-verify.sh`, modeled on the prototype
`tests/tasks/e-1577-verify.sh` (its shared `assert_*` / `report_pass` / `report_fail` helpers,
section headers, a summary with ALL PASSED, exit 0 on all-pass / 1 on any failure). It asserts:

- `endless verify <id>` on a sample task with a passing suite → exit 0, CTRF written, summary shown.
- a deliberately failing suite → non-zero exit, failure detail surfaced.
- the suite runs with `HOME`/`XDG_CONFIG_HOME` pointed at temp dirs — confirm it cannot see the
  developer's real config.
- two concurrent runs don't collide.
- `--keep` retains the temp dir; default removes it.

Interim location `tests/tasks/` (matches the existing prototypes); migrate to the formal
`.endless/tasks/<id>/` convention once the system lands.

# Plan — vendored CTRF-subset result writer + native normalizers

Shared interface contract is in the epic's analysis (`endless task show E-1596 --analysis`,
section "Interface Contract / C" and locked decision 1). Conform to the CTRF-subset shape; don't
invent fields.

## Deliver

1. **A Go CTRF-subset writer** — structs for the subset in decision/contract C: `results.tool`,
   `results.summary {tests,passed,failed,skipped,pending,other,start,stop}`, `results.tests[]`
   with per-test `name`/`status`/`duration` (+ optional `message`, `trace`, `suite`,
   `stdout`/`stderr`, `attachments[] {name,contentType,path}`, `extra`). Field names/shapes
   match CTRF; pin to the CTRF snapshot (2025-11-24). Emit a single JSON document.
2. **Native normalizers** keyed by `format`:
   - `gotest-json`: parse the `go test -json` TestEvent stream (Action/Test/Elapsed/Output).
   - `pytest-json`: parse pytest's JSON report.
   - `tap`: parse TAP (for shell/BATS suites).
   Each maps native → the CTRF-subset structs.
3. **No runtime dependency on external CTRF reporters** — Endless reads the native streams and
   writes CTRF itself. (Don't add `go-ctrf-json-reporter`/`pytest-json-ctrf` as runtime deps.)

## Notes

- Keep the subset minimal; add CTRF fields only when a concrete need appears.
- The `attachments[]`/`extra` hooks exist for non-text outcomes (screenshots, HTTP transcripts)
  but need no producer yet.
- Consumed by E-1603; used to render expected/got failure detail.

## Verify — ad-hoc verify script (interim, E-1577 style)

Until the formal verification system this epic builds is usable, verify this task with a
committed ad-hoc script `tests/tasks/e-1604-verify.sh`, modeled on the prototype
`tests/tasks/e-1577-verify.sh` (same shared helpers / section headers / ALL-PASSED summary /
exit 0 on all-pass, 1 on any failure). It asserts:

- a known `go test -json` stream, a pytest JSON sample, and a TAP sample each normalize to the
  expected CTRF-subset JSON (golden comparison).
- failures carry `message`/`trace`; summary counts match the native totals.
- output validates as a single JSON object with the pinned CTRF field names.

The underlying assertions may be Go golden tests the script invokes; the script is the uniform
pass/fail entry point. Interim location `tests/tasks/` (matches the existing prototypes); migrate
to the formal `.endless/tasks/<id>/` convention once the system lands.

# E-1612 — research scope

Investigate the verification blind spot observed while writing the E-1572 ad-hoc verify script
(`tests/tasks/e-1572-verify.sh`): the combination of (a) advisory code that swallows its own
failures and (b) dependency resolution that can silently pick a stale artifact, which together
let a verify suite report ALL PASSED while the feature is actually broken.

Characterize the hazard precisely, then recommend concrete mitigations the E-1596
verification-suite framework should adopt. Deliver findings + recommendations in this task's
**outcome**.
